title: 👑 Elite Challenge: Passive Voice Across Tenses & Modals

questions.prompt:
Transform to passive (future simple): "The government will announce the results tomorrow."
Which option is correct?

options.text: The results will announced by the government tomorrow.
The results are announced by the government tomorrow.
The results will be announced by the government tomorrow.
The results were announced by the government tomorrow.

questions.explanation: Future simple passive = will + be + past participle. "Announce" → past participle "announced". The object "the results" becomes the subject: The results WILL BE ANNOUNCED by the government tomorrow. Option a omits "be"; b uses present passive (wrong tense); d uses past passive (wrong tense).

questions.prompt:
Transform to passive (present perfect): "Someone has stolen the exam papers."
Which option is correct?

options.text: The exam papers have been stolen.
The exam papers has been stolen.
The exam papers were stolen.
The exam papers had been stolen.

questions.explanation: Present perfect passive = have / has + been + past participle. "The exam papers" is plural → HAVE (not has). By-agent is omitted because the agent (someone) is unknown. Option b incorrectly uses "has" with a plural subject; c is past simple (loses the present-perfect meaning); d is past perfect (wrong tense).

questions.prompt:
Transform to passive (modal — must): "You must submit all assignments before Friday."
Which option is correct?

options.text: All assignments must been submitted before Friday.
All assignments must be submitted before Friday.
All assignments must being submitted before Friday.
All assignments must submitted before Friday.

questions.explanation: Passive with a modal = modal + be + past participle. "Must" is already a base-form modal, so: must + BE + submitted. Option a uses "been" without "be" (wrong); c uses "being" (continuous form, wrong here); d omits "be" entirely.

questions.prompt:
Turn the active question into a passive question (present simple): "Do they teach Arabic in this school?"
Which passive question is correct?

options.text: Are Arabic taught in this school?
Does Arabic taught in this school?
Was Arabic taught in this school?
Is Arabic taught in this school?

questions.explanation: Present simple passive question = Is / Are + subject + past participle? "Arabic" is an uncountable/singular noun → IS (not are). Past participle of teach = taught. Option a wrongly uses "Are" with the singular noun "Arabic"; b keeps the active auxiliary "Does" (wrong); c uses "Was" — past tense, not present.

questions.prompt:
Active: "Doctors should check patients regularly."
Which passive sentence is correct AND uses the most natural style (agent omitted when obvious)?

options.text: Patients should be check regularly.
Patients should been checked regularly.
Patients should be checked regularly.
Patients should being checked regularly.

questions.explanation: Modal passive = should + be + past participle (checked). The agent "by doctors" is omitted because it is obvious from context. Option a uses the base form "check" instead of the past participle; b omits "be" and has "been" in the wrong position; d uses "being" (a progressive form that does not follow a bare modal).

questions.prompt:
Active: "The jury awarded the winner a gold medal."
Which passive sentence is correct?

options.text: The winner were awarded a gold medal by the jury.
The winner was awarded a gold medal by the jury.
A gold medal is awarded to the winner by the jury.
The winner was awarded to a gold medal by the jury.

questions.explanation: With two-object verbs like "award", the indirect object (the winner) can become the subject: The winner WAS awarded a gold medal by the jury. "The winner" is singular + past tense → WAS (not were). Option a wrongly uses "were" with a singular subject; c uses present tense (wrong) and misplaces "to"; d incorrectly inserts "to" between "awarded" and "a gold medal", which is ungrammatical in this pattern.
